finding: A behavioral practice (language switching) conferred an advantage on a structurally similar task (task switching), showing better global performance but not local performance.
An outcome perceived as rare is reconstructed as having originated from a longer sequence than an outcome perceived as common, with perceptual rarity mattering more than actual rarity.
Salience of a negative self-directed consequence (regret) reduced a decision bias, whereas salience of external evaluation demands maintained or exacerbated the bias.
A causal explanation for an unexplained event was more effective at correcting misinformation than denial alone, even when denial was backed by strong evidence.
Activation of gender stereotypes reduced the performance of individuals from the stereotyped group on a knowledge task.
Contemplation of abstract personality traits related to interpersonal warmth influenced perception of ambient temperature.
A comorbid condition amplified the severity of criminal behavior and weakened emotional responses in individuals with a baseline disorder.
Three procedures reduced commitment to a failing course of action: reducing threat of negative outcomes, setting minimum target thresholds, and evaluating decision process rather than outcome.
Two personality traits predicted leadership behavior when measured broadly, but their facet-level components showed weaker predictive validity.
The temporal valence of regret reversed: actions produced more regret short-term, but inactions produced more regret long-term.
A mortality salience effect on intergroup bias occurred only when the salient thoughts were accessible but outside conscious awareness.
A social orientation was predicted by early childhood social structures and increased in prevalence across the adult lifespan.
Exertion of self-control on one task impaired self-control on subsequent tasks, consistent with a resource-depletion mechanism.
Cultural values shaped individual preferences for novel versus conventional options, with the relationship bidirectional (values influence preferences and preferences reinforce values).
A personality configuration measured in midlife mediated the influence of earlier personality on later mental health outcomes.
Public exposure of an event was more strongly associated with one emotion than another, whereas private aspects were more strongly associated with the other emotion.
Stereotypical association of a domain with an outgroup identity reduced identification with that domain in members of a different group, even when they had chosen the domain professionally.
Childhood personality predicted adult personality, and a broad adult personality trait was linked to maladaptation across childhood and adulthood.

Members of one cultural group showed dispositional attribution bias regardless of behavioral diagnosticity, whereas members of another cultural group showed the bias only when the behavior was diagnostic of attitude.
A personality framework predicted complex behavioral outcomes consistently across independent samples and multiple measurement instruments.
